Redislocated the radial head and retrieved the trial. We tamped in place our final implant. I closed the extensor wad with a running locking stitch attached to the G2 Mitek anchor which was buried in his condyle. I ran and locked this to repair the deep fascia as well as the superficial fascia overlying the radial head. 2-0 Vicryl was used. Subcutaneous tissue followed by 2- 0 Prolene. Sterile dressing was placed. He was placed in a sling. He was extubated and transferred to the PACU in stable condition. The patient is considered homebound because the patient has recent displaced fractures of the head and neck of the right radius; with recent surgery and a non-removable surgical dressing to the right elbow and requires the assistance of another person to safely leave the home. Objective findings supporting homebound status include postoperative right elbow wound not observable due to non-removable dressing, frequent activity-provoked pain of the right elbow/shoulder (pain 3/10 with activity), documented need to maintain limb rest and support during movement, and a documented fall risk (MAHC score 5) while the patient lives alone. Leaving home would require a considerable and taxing effort because transfers, car entry/exit, negotiating steps/curbs or uneven surfaces, and maintaining wound/dressing integrity would provoke pain, risk wound disruption or infection, and exceed the patient's current functional capacity without human assistance; skilled staff documented the patient "needs assistance to leave the place of residence." Allowable absences are limited to brief, medically necessary outings (for example, physician or wound care/therapy appointments); extended or routine community outings are unsafe at this time. These objectively documented functional limitations, postoperative wound care needs, pain and impaired mobility demonstrate that the need for medical necessity for skilled home health services (skilled nursing for wound/pain management and safety monitoring, and skilled therapy for mobility and transfer training) to support safe recovery in the home setting. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN. Patient is a 38-year-old male admitted to Meadows Home Health. The patient is considered homebound because the patient has recent displaced fractures of the head and neck of the right radius; with recent surgery and a non-removable surgical dressing to the right elbow and requires the assistance of another person to safely leave the home. Objective findings supporting homebound status include postoperative right elbow wound not observable due to non-removable dressing, frequent activity-provoked pain of the right elbow/shoulder (pain 3/10 with activity), documented need to maintain limb rest and support during movement, and a documented fall risk (MAHC score 5) while the patient lives alone. Leaving home would require a considerable and taxing effort because transfers, car entry/exit, negotiating steps/curbs or uneven surfaces, and maintaining wound/dressing integrity would provoke pain, risk wound disruption or infection, and exceed the patient's current functional capacity without human assistance; skilled staff documented the patient "needs assistance to leave the place of residence." Allowable absences are limited to brief, medically necessary outings (for example, physician or wound care/therapy appointments); extended or routine community outings are unsafe at this time. These objectively documented functional limitations, postoperative wound care needs, pain and impaired mobility demonstrate that the need for medical necessity for skilled home health services (skilled nursing for wound/pain management and safety monitoring, and skilled therapy for mobility and transfer training) to support safe recovery in the home setting. Frequency and duration: HH SN frequency: 1 time a week for 4 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education. HH PT frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week for 3 weeks evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training effective on 03/27/2026. Goals: -Reduce patient-reported pain at the right elbow/shoulder to <2/10 at rest and with activity using current analgesic regimen and prescribed nonpharmacologic measures (ice, positioning, relaxation) as reported by the patient, within 4 weeks. -Demonstrate improved functional mobility by achieving sit-to-stand transfers with supervision or minimal assistance and ambulating at least 50 feet with the prescribed assistive device (or wheeling 50 feet if using wheelchair) as observed by PT or RN, within 4 weeks. -Patient and/or caregiver will correctly perform wound care procedures (including hand hygiene, dressing management, and infection-warning signs) and complete a return demonstration with ≥80% accuracy, within 4 weeks. -Increase independence with self-care: patient will complete upper-body dressing with setup assistance only and manage toileting hygiene with supervision or setup assistance, as measured by RN/PT observation, within 4 weeks. -Patient and/or caregiver will accurately demonstrate medication management (name, dose, frequency, indications, and when to call clinician) and complete medication reconciliation with RN documentation, within 4 weeks. Teachings/Interventions Skilled Intervention Assessment/Instruction/Performance: Skilled Nurse instructed the following: Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment on all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharge medications against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed plan of care draft with patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial discussion on the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit. Skilled Nurse assessed the patient's mobility and functional status related to fracture of head of right radius and neck of right radius. Skilled Nurse assessed the environment for safety at each visit to reduce the risk of falls or accidents. Skilled Nurse instructed the patient/caregiver on the importance of monitoring conditions and risk factors that could contribute to falls, such as fatigue and muscle weakness. Skilled Nurse assessed the patient's skin for breakdown at every visit due to decreased mobility associated with the fracture. Skilled Nurse assessed peripheral circulation, sensation, and motor function, especially in the affected extremity. Skilled Nurse encouraged the patient to mobilize as tolerated to promote blood circulation. Skilled Nurse instructed the patient/caregiver to maintain limb rest as indicated and to provide support to the joints above and below the fracture site, particularly during movement and repositioning. Skilled Nurse instructed the patient/caregiver on infection control techniques, including proper handwashing, use of gloves, and maintaining good personal hygiene. Skilled Nurse taught the patient/caregiver fall prevention strategies, including the proper use of assistive devices and keeping personal items within arm's reach. Skilled Nurse reinforced the importance of avoiding tasks beyond the patient's current functional ability and requesting assistance when needed. Skilled nurse assessed the patient's pain level using an appropriate pain scale and noted that the patient experienced pain rated at 3/10 during the assessment. The nurse evaluated the effectiveness of current pain management strategies and instructed the patient to adhere to and take pain medication before pain becomes severe to achieve optimal pain control. Provided teachings on nonpharmacologic pain relief measures such as relaxation techniques and positioning. Instructed patient and caregiver to notify the skilled nurse or medical doctor if the patient experiences pain greater than 7/10, is unable to tolerate pain, or if pain is affecting the ability to perform normal activities and ADLs. The skilled nurse guided the patient in performing gentle movements and exercises within tolerance to enhance circulation and prevent stiffness, which aided in pain reduction. Following interventions, the patient reported a reduction in pain level and demonstrated improved comfort. Skilled Nurse assessed patient's safety in environment, mobility and functional limitations to reduce falls or accidents every visit. Assessed the patient's ability to perform ADLs effectively and safely every visit. Instructed Patient/Caregiver on the importance of assessing conditions/risks that may contribute to occurrence of falls such as fatigue/ muscle weakness. Encouraged to perform exercises if tolerated for strengthening and maintenance of posture and flexibility. Home exercise program (HEP) was established, and patient/caregiver were educated on passive and active assistive range- of-motion (ROM) exercises.
